title: Fetch a secret with the Python SDK
summary: Install the oneclaw Python SDK, create a client, and print a secret from your vault.

blocks.text: The 1Claw Python SDK ships as the PyPI package oneclaw. You create a client with an API key, and it handles token exchange and refresh for you.
You need Python 3.9+ and a 1Claw API key from 1claw.xyz/settings/api-keys. A 1ck_ key is a user key, an ocv_ key is an agent key. Both work here.

steps.text: Create and activate a virtual environment.
Install the SDK from PyPI.

Export your API key and the vault ID that holds a secret. Replace `1ck_your_user_key` with your 1ck_ key from Settings → API Keys. Use your vault UUID from `1claw vault list` or the Foundations track.
Write the following script and save it as `fetch_secret.py`. It creates a client and reads one secret by path.
steps.code:
import os
from oneclaw import create_client

client = create_client(api_key=os.environ["ONECLAW_API_KEY"])
vault_id = os.environ["ONECLAW_VAULT_ID"]

secret = client.secrets.get(vault_id, "api-keys/openai")
print(secret.data["value"])

steps.text: Create the secret if it doesn't already exist.
Run it. The fetched secret value prints to your terminal.

blocks.text: Use create_client() inside a with block if you want the connection pool closed automatically when the script exits.
You now have a Python script that authenticates to 1Claw and prints a real secret pulled from your vault.

quiz.question: What is the PyPI package name for the 1Claw Python SDK?
quiz.explanation: The SDK installs with pip install oneclaw.

quiz.question: Which call retrieves a single secret by path?
quiz.explanation: The README shows client.secrets.get(vault_id, path) returning the value under secret.data['value'].

quiz.question: What does an ocv_ prefixed key represent?
quiz.explanation: ocv_ keys are agent keys, while 1ck_ keys are user keys.

title: Wire an AI client to the 1Claw MCP server
summary: Configure Claude Desktop, Cursor, or VS Code to reach the 1Claw MCP server and call list_secrets.

blocks.text: The 1Claw MCP server gives an AI client tools to list, get, and manage vault secrets. You can use the hosted server at mcp.1claw.xyz or run a local stdio process.

Prerequisites: a 1Claw account, a vault, an agent with an ocv_ API key, and a policy granting that agent read access to the secret paths it needs. Create an agent in the Agents & Access Control track, or via `POST /v1/agents`. Copy agent_id and api_key from the creation response.

steps.text: Option A: Local stdio (recommended for development). Add the 1claw MCP server to your client config. Only ONECLAW_AGENT_API_KEY is required. Agent ID and vault are auto-discovered. Paste your real ocv_ key from agent creation in place of the placeholder.
Option B: Hosted server. First get a JWT by calling the agent-token endpoint. Replace the placeholders with your real agent_id and ocv_ key from agent creation.
steps.code:
export AGENT_ID="paste-your-agent-uuid-here"
export AGENT_KEY="ocv_paste_your_agent_key_here"

TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST https://api.1claw.xyz/v1/auth/agent-token \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d "{\"agent_id\":\"$AGENT_ID\",\"api_key\":\"$AGENT_KEY\"}" | jq -r '.access_token')

export VAULT_ID="paste-your-vault-uuid-here"

steps.text: Use that token in a hosted MCP config. Claude Desktop, Cursor, and Claude Code all use this same shape. Replace $TOKEN and $VAULT_ID with the values from the previous step.
Restart the client, then ask it to list your secrets.
steps.code: List the secrets in my 1claw vault.

blocks.text: Config file locations — Claude Desktop: ~/Library/Application Support/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json (macOS). Cursor: Settings → MCP Servers. VS Code: .vscode/mcp.json.
The hosted JWT expires in about an hour. The local stdio server exchanges the ocv_ key for a JWT and refreshes it automatically, so it is simpler for long sessions.
Your AI client now calls list_secrets against your vault and returns the secret paths and metadata.

quiz.question: Which single environment variable is required for the local stdio MCP server?
quiz.explanation: Only ONECLAW_AGENT_API_KEY is required; agent ID and vault are auto-discovered from the token exchange.

quiz.question: How do you get the Bearer token for the hosted MCP server?
options: By calling POST /v1/auth/agent-token with agent_id and api_key
It is the ocv_ key itself
quiz.explanation: You POST your agent_id and api_key to /v1/auth/agent-token and use the returned access_token.

quiz.question: Which tool confirms the connection works?
quiz.explanation: Asking the client to list secrets triggers list_secrets and verifies the setup.

title: Give a LangChain agent vault access
summary: Wrap the 1Claw SDK in a LangChain tool so an agent fetches a vault secret on demand.

blocks.text: This example lets a LangChain agent decide when to list and fetch vault secrets. You define custom tools that wrap @1claw/sdk, and the LLM calls them just in time so raw values never sit in the prompt.
Prerequisites: Node.js 20+, a 1Claw account with a vault containing at least one secret, and one LLM key (OPENAI_API_KEY or a Gemini free tier GOOGLE_API_KEY).

steps.text: Clone the examples repository and install dependencies.
steps.code:
git clone https://github.com/1clawAI/1claw-examples.git
cd 1claw-examples/langchain-agent
npm install
cp .env.example .env

steps.text: Fill in .env with your 1Claw and LLM credentials. `npm start` uses ONECLAW_API_KEY (user key), while `npm run mcp` uses ONECLAW_AGENT_API_KEY (agent key).
If your vault is empty, add a test secret with the CLI.
Note the tool pattern: a DynamicStructuredTool wraps the SDK client so the agent can list secrets. BASE_URL, API_KEY, and VAULT_ID are defined from environment variables at the top of the file.
Run the tool-calling agent.

blocks.text: Run `npm run mcp` instead to connect LangChain to the hosted 1Claw MCP server, which loads all 27+ vault tools with zero manual tool definitions. That path needs ONECLAW_AGENT_API_KEY and OPENAI_API_KEY.
Your LangChain agent now lists vault secrets through the SDK tool and reports paths and types without leaking the value.

quiz.question: How does the agent avoid putting raw secret values in the prompt?
options: It fetches secrets just in time via tools and the system prompt forbids echoing values
quiz.explanation: The LLM calls vault tools on demand and is instructed never to reveal raw secret values.

quiz.question: Which package do the custom LangChain tools wrap?
quiz.explanation: The tool func calls createClient from @1claw/sdk.

quiz.question: What does npm run mcp do differently from npm start?
options: It connects to the hosted 1Claw MCP server and loads all vault tools automatically
quiz.explanation: The MCP client script connects to mcp.1claw.xyz and gets all 27+ tools without defining them by hand.

title: Give a CrewAI crew secure secret access
summary: Use the 1claw-crewai-tools package so a CrewAI agent fetches a secret at runtime.

blocks.text: The 1claw-crewai-tools package adds an OneclawVaultTool that fetches credentials from your vault at runtime using a scoped agent identity. Secrets are not copied into prompts, repos, or long-lived memory.

The PyPI distribution name is 1claw-crewai-tools. The Python import package is oneclaw_crewai. You also need an agent bound to a vault with a read policy on the secret path, plus an LLM key. Requires an agent with a read policy on the secret path. See the Agents & Access Control track for setup.

steps.text: Install the tools package and CrewAI.
Export the agent identity, vault, and an LLM key. Use values from agent creation (see Agents & Access Control track) and your vault UUID from `1claw vault list`.
Build a crew that gives one agent the vault tool. Save the script as `crew_demo.py`.
steps.code:
import os
from crewai import Agent, Crew, Process, Task
from oneclaw_crewai import OneclawVaultTool

vault_tool = OneclawVaultTool(
    agent_id=os.environ["ONECLAW_AGENT_ID"],
    api_key=os.environ["ONECLAW_AGENT_API_KEY"],
    vault_id=os.environ["ONECLAW_VAULT_ID"],
)

agent = Agent(
    role="Engineer",
    goal="Build features using vault-stored API keys",
    backstory="You use tools instead of pasted secrets.",
    tools=[vault_tool],
    verbose=False,
)

task = Task(
    description="Read path api-keys/openai using the vault tool; do not echo raw values.",
    expected_output="Confirmation that the path was read.",
    agent=agent,
)

crew = Crew(agents=[agent], tasks=[task], process=Process.sequential)
crew.kickoff()

steps.text: Create the secret if it doesn't already exist.
Run the crew.

blocks.text: Keep verbose=False in production. With verbose=True, CrewAI logs the raw return value of every tool call, which prints secret values to stdout. Never print or log the tool return value.
Your crew now fetches a secret from 1Claw through the oneclaw_vault tool during a task, without the value ever landing in code or repo.

quiz.question: What is the import package name for the CrewAI tools?
quiz.explanation: You pip install 1claw-crewai-tools but import from oneclaw_crewai.

quiz.question: Why should verbose stay False in production?
options: CrewAI logs raw tool return values, which can print secrets
quiz.explanation: With verbose=True, CrewAI's executor prints the raw return value of every tool call, including secret values.

quiz.question: How are the vault credentials retrieved?
options: At import time from a config file
At runtime using a scoped agent identity
quiz.explanation: Credentials are fetched at runtime with a scoped agent identity, not baked into prompts or repos.

title: Load the 1Claw plugin into an elizaOS agent
summary: Bootstrap a vault and agent, then configure @1claw/plugin-elizaos in a character.

blocks.text: The @1claw/plugin-elizaos package gives an elizaOS character HSM-backed vault access and multi-chain signing. It wraps @1claw/sdk and exposes actions like GET_SECRET, LIST_SECRETS, and SIGN_MESSAGE.

Use a human 1ck_ API key only during bootstrap to provision resources. The bootstrap script never stores the human key. It writes an agent-only ocv_ key for your character. If you don't have an existing elizaOS project, see the elizaOS quickstart at https://elizaos.github.io/eliza/docs/quickstart to set one up first.

steps.text: Clone the plugin repo and install.
Run bootstrap with your human key to create a vault, agent, and policy.
Bootstrap writes .env.elizaos with agent credentials. Validate them.
Add the plugin to your character file. Copy ONECLAW_AGENT_API_KEY, ONECLAW_AGENT_ID, and ONECLAW_VAULT_ID from .env.elizaos into settings.secrets in your character file.
Alternative: add to an existing project. If you already have an elizaOS project, install the plugin directly instead of cloning the repo.
Start your elizaOS agent and verify the 1claw plugin loaded. You should see a log line confirming the plugin registered its actions.

blocks.text: Pass ONECLAW_ENABLE_INTENTS=true to the bootstrap command if you want transaction signing enabled on the agent at create time.
Your elizaOS agent now boots with the 1Claw plugin loaded, authenticates with its ocv_ key, and can run vault and signing actions.

quiz.question: What is the human 1ck_ key used for in this flow?
options: Setup only, to provision the vault, agent, and policy
quiz.explanation: The human key is used only during bootstrap and is never written to disk; the character runs on the agent ocv_ key.

quiz.question: Which file does bootstrap write agent credentials to?
quiz.explanation: Bootstrap writes ONECLAW_AGENT_API_KEY, ONECLAW_AGENT_ID, and ONECLAW_VAULT_ID to .env.elizaos with mode 600.

quiz.question: Where do private signing keys live?
options: In the HSM or TEE, never leaving it
quiz.explanation: Private signing keys never leave the HSM/TEE; the agent submits intents and 1Claw signs server-side.

title: Sign onchain with 1Claw AgentKit on Base
summary: Run the 1Claw AgentKit MCP server so an autonomous agent signs Base transactions with no keys on disk.

blocks.text: @1claw/agentkit is a hardened MCP server built on Coinbase AgentKit. Your agent gets the full onchain toolkit for Base, but signing happens in a TEE via the Intents API and secrets are resolved from your vault at boot, never touching disk.
This path is for autonomous agents that run without a human approving each transaction. Guardrails like per-tx caps, daily limits, and address allowlists are enforced server-side.

steps.text: Path A: MCP server setup. Clone the repo and run the setup wizard. It asks for your human 1ck_ key and provisions a vault, an agent with Intents API and Base guardrails, a Base signing key, and a read policy on agentkit/*.
steps.code:
git clone https://github.com/1clawAI/1claw-agentkit.git
cd 1claw-agentkit
npm install
npm run setup

steps.text: The wizard prints an MCP config JSON. Paste the JSON the wizard prints into your AI client config. It pairs 1claw-agentkit with the 1claw MCP server under the agent key the wizard provisioned.
Set the chain ID for Base Sepolia (testnet) before moving to mainnet.

Path B: Library API. Resolve secrets and build a Base wallet provider, then send a TEE-signed transaction. Use the agent credentials from the `npm run setup` wizard. Value is in ETH (0.001 ETH ≈ a few dollars).
Path C: Full-stack starter (Scaffold-Agent). To bootstrap a full-stack onchain dApp instead, use Scaffold-Agent, which wires HSM-backed secrets and Intents signing into a Scaffold-ETH 2 scaffold.

blocks.text: Set ONECLAW_CHAIN_ID to 84532 for Base Sepolia (testnet) or 8453 for Base mainnet. Always test on Sepolia first.
Your agent now signs and broadcasts transactions on Base through the Intents API, with keys held in the vault TEE and guardrails capping what a prompt injection could ever do.

quiz.question: Where are the signing keys stored with @1claw/agentkit?
options: In the 1Claw Vault with HSM and MPC, never on disk
quiz.explanation: Credentials resolve from the HSM-encrypted vault at boot and never touch disk; signing happens in a TEE.

quiz.question: When should you pick @1claw/agentkit over mcp.base.org?
options: When a human approves every transaction
When the agent runs autonomously with no human per transaction
quiz.explanation: @1claw/agentkit is for autonomous agents with programmatic guardrails, while mcp.base.org suits human-in-the-loop use.

quiz.question: What starter kit bootstraps a full onchain dApp with 1Claw?
quiz.explanation: Scaffold-Agent wires HSM-backed secrets and Intents API signing into a Scaffold-ETH 2 scaffold.
